item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love the ambiguity here. I really can't decide on my own headcanon.
Shapeshifting spy(and at least 2 variations that aren't spies)? Interdimensional travel? Actual direct duplication by some means? Who knows?

Also love the way the relationship developed between the two.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there any reason it couldn't be temporal (or extradimensional, along similar lines)? Going from:

Baltimore: Look at it this way: it, uh, doubles us. It's a perfect copy. It thinks it's us. It's 2230 hours, it's tired, it goes back to our quarters, just as I did earlier. Or you did later. Maybe it wasn't coming to attack us. It's just coming home to get some rest.

They both seem to believe they are real. Maybe the reason they are are so indistinguishable is that one has (unknowingly) slipped out of another time stream, resulting in there being two iterations of Dr. F██████ present now.

All we know about Nancy Y█████ is that she cannot be found. It seems possible that she has also been temporally displaced, and is currently being contained by some parallel Foundation.

It being a metamorphic entity seems like a big assumption, unless there's something I've overlooked.

+Nitpicks/suggestions
-Hide

Lawrence: This is investigator Lawrence. The time is now 707 hours

This might be clearer with a leading 0 (so as "0707 hours"). I initially misread it as meaning 707 hours have passed.

Lawrence: I'll oblige you. If you are Dr F██████

This should be "Dr. F█████", as "Dr." is used in all other instances. I've edited that as it appears to be just a typo.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from AP as indicates here, and thus is non-compliant. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: Easy +1
item.content:encoded:
Clear, though-provoking concept, transcripts make it personal, somber tone feels appropriate. Loved it. Two nitpicks from the recovery log:

  • I agree with the earlier comment about "non-zero probability" sounding unrealistic. I'd expect something like "probability above acceptable baseline". I wouldn't say it bothered me but it did stick out.
  • There's a "sometime" in the third paragraph that I think should be "some time". Might fix that one once I've been around a little longer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know, responding to a very old post, but this needs to be said.

This is a setting where things that violate natural laws not only exist, but are implicitly quite commonplace away from the eyes of the general populace. And the Foundation regularly makes use of science and technology that is either explicitly anomalous in nature or is simply far more "advanced" than what modern-day human society is capable of, such as drugs that make you forget whatever the Foundation wants you to forget (it's often implied that the induced amnesia can be selective). It's not outside the realm of possibility that the Foundation has methods/tools of anomalous/super-advanced nature that genuinely make torture a relatively viable method of interrogation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This should be much higher. This thing is what serious non horror scips should strive to achieve!

Love it to death. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dug it. Except for the 'Kafkaesque' part, which I thought referred to bizarre, indecipherable systems, not logical traps. (Which I guess you address with the Dostoevsky line?)

Anyway the situation feels more to me like a reverse version of the prisoners dilemma - the only way for anyone to win in the situation is for someone to admit they did it, it's just that if they do, they lose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing I wonder is.

WHO THE HELL IS THE IMPOSTOR, A OR B?

Nevermind…I know there's no anwser. Never.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sort of meh but the interview logs were good

+1

item.title: Re: The feels...
item.content:encoded: Wow, thanks very much. Thats what I was going for. The mournful

item.title: The feels...
item.content:encoded:
The sad SCPs always get me, because there are so few of them.

I feel for this guy, and the strange circumstances in which he's found himself, where it is impossible for him to definitively prove his innocence. The meaningless posthumous honor planned for him is a nice touch. This is one of the best of Series III.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would add to that by saying as Kalinin says that the foundation seems unrealistically hell bent on continuing to apply torture even after it has become clear that it won't work. The flip side of that though is that much of the drama and humanity of the story is related to the Dr's and SCP's experiences surviving torture. Rather than toning down this element, I would suggest providing the Foundation with a stronger rationale to keep trying it.

Also the fact that the foundation freaked out after discovering a "non-zero" probability that they had been infiltrated is absurd. Inferential statistics doesn't work like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how the SCP itself doesn't realize it's a duplicate and how the two Doctors bond through the interrogations. The only gripe I have is that sorry excuse for a description. I mean, I know the focus of the SCP is the Addenda, but it's so short and not very informational.
Upvoted nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you very much. I'm glad it reads better now. Its basically a sad story on one level, a comedy on another.

-edit

i'll look for that Lem book

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After the edits, I like it a lot better. The foundation are their old "cold but not cruel" selves, and there's enough hints to make for a number of intriguing backstories.

The way I read it, there is no imposter - just a duplication, and the foundation is slightly too habitually paranoid to follow that possibility.

I'm reminded a little of Stanislaw Lem's "The Investigation", where a police inspector is determined to find the criminal responsible for a series of inexplicable events…"even if he doesn't exist".

So…my initial impulsive downvote is hereby reversed. First time I've ever done that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks. nothing wrong with a little Stoppard

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally like the little rozencrantz and guildinstien tidbit in the converstions they had,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In-universe, this is a life-or-death situation for the Foundation, and the narrative seeks to underline just how useless it is.

So the Foundation resorts to using methods it knows won't work…because it can't come up with methods that will?

Sodium Pentathol? Hypnosis? Blackmail? Threatening the researcher's family? Seeing as this is the foundation, they could use a "Mission Impossible" plot to convince 2054 that the entire foundation had already been replaced with replicants.

Aren't there any skips that can test for veracity? Or do super-advanced bioscans? Or just force a person to tell the truth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's. I'm glad you liked that. =D. I tried to show an evolution to their relationship. I line the direction it went.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just read this, and this is the first and only version of this that I've read, and I like it very much as it is now. I especially like how after the years they start to refer to themselves as "we" and to the entity as "it", as if there's three of them and not two.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: exactly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's funny you say that. I was imagining a probability phenomenon. He's in statistics, after all.

-edit
The more I've thought about your idea the more I've liked it. I've tried to incorporate it in my rewrite. Perhaps the researcher who disappeared went in the other direction. Over in the duplicate universe they've quarantined two copied of her.

I particularly like the idea of an alternate but identical universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my headcannon this is just a slight variation on 507. If it truly is an mole, then the trigger/thing controlling it is hidden far too deep to be detectable.

What I think really happened is the guy who saw the light was somehow transported to our universe, a universe that was completely identical to ours. The only difference being is that now there are two versions of him. This small cosmic accident has caused the Foundation a decade of work, along with taking out an employee of theirs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's what I was hoping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi all. I've tried to take the criticism to heart and made some changes to address it. Sorry if I bristled, or was defensive.

One thing I'd love to have people's opinion on. Say the torture angle is still too harsh. What would be a more natural time line as far as the Foundation's response?

For instance:
1. initial alarm
2. quarantine
3. Nancy Y█████ is discovered absent.
3. colleagues commiserate. advocates are appointed to both of them
4. experimentation, medical, physical, psychological, etc.
5. longer term confinement.
6. interrogation….

then what? Or what other stages in between?

Anyway, thanks =D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I think the torture might have been justified - not so much as an interrogation method, but in the hope that they might force the copy to either revert to its original form (assuming it has one) or assume a new form. In either event, they can then be certain which one is the real researcher, and which one is the doppelganger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For that matter, is it the impostor? Or is it in fact the original researcher, still desperately hoping to convince his colleagues that the scip is neutralized?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kalinin, Wei, and Ael perfectly echo my thoughts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks.

-edit I tried to make the third one more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except that torture never gives good information. People will say whatever they think their torturer wants to hear, to make the pain stop. It's been documented again and again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ditto. There's no sense involved here at all, and rather than being dramatic tension, anyone who knows better is simply wondering why the people involved are being so pants-on-head stupid about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure the new third interview adds much, but the final piece of the two of them talking definitely does. Still enjoying this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Strongly seconding Kalinin here. The torture aspect seems like a needless method of containment designed to give this an added squick factor that doesn't make sense from a logical perspective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In-universe, this is a life-or-death situation for the Foundation, and the narrative seeks to underline just how useless it is.

So this is a "gee the Foundation sure is dumb" type thing then. I've gone into great detail elsewhere about why I don't care for that, and I'm generally baffled as to why this is apparently a thing now.

Mere containment can not be an option. The SCP, as I keep pointing out, has been masquerading for decades. Its imperative that any/all measures be employed to discover what it was doing, what it was up to.

I'm not getting the sense of panic that you're trying to convey here, that apparently is supposed to justify the Foundation doing horrible things to its own employees for no good reason. The skip is either dead, or it's been in "mere containment" for an extended period with no reasonably hope of escape. I don't see the problem for containment there.

But say the double has boobytrapped all sorts of Keter-class items. Say it has brethren in the O5 council. Say its in the thrall of some GOI. What would you do in that situation?

Those seem to be questions that could be answered through investigative techniques. We know it was impersonating a specific researcher. Surely one could look through all the work that he's done and see if something's not right. Then you can investigate personnel he's come into contact with, skips he's worked on, etc. That is surely, from a scientific perspective, a more fruitful avenue of research, and it doesn't look to have even been discussed.

To be clear, I don't mean this as a tacit endorsement of real-world equivalents like Guatanamo Bay, Abu Grhaib, etc. This is a story. Not advocacy.

At no point did I claim you were advocating for torture. My problem isn't that you shouldn't write about morally questionable things, my problem is that it makes no sense here.

this is the only way to safely ever release the real Doctor. Conceivably, they could give him amnesties to remove the memory of the torture.

Based on the way you've written this, there is no conceivable way it would ever be safe to release the doctor, since there is no means of verifying that he isn't the skip. Again, why torture him? So he can make dubious claims that you can't fact-check anyway? By the internal logic of your own article, he has to stay in confinement. Also, since you're relying on the moral quandry and psychological effects thereof to carry this article, amnesticizing the guy at the end seems like an extremely cheap way out narrative-wise.

I'm not sure what you refer to when you mention inconsistencies. Please let me know what you mean.

I, the reader, am supposed to be affected by the terrible, unjust treatment of the staff member in the story, right? So why is everyone, including the doctor himself, talking about this like it's a boring assignment that got foisted on someone on a Friday? I may not care for the dramatic device you're using, but the sort of 'ho-hum' way everyone is approaching it is undercutting what you're attempting to do. That's inconsistency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something I think is missing: what did they do with the dead one's remains? I imagine they are in permanent containment. Even if "diffuse light-emitting entity" is coming from the fake doctor, the foundation does not know this thing's nature or whether it can reanimate.

Edit: also, I always assume that any mention of enhanced interrogation techniques does not refer to simple torture, but something more advanced and maybe partially anomalous. Physical torture might be a component, but there are additional components which make it actually work. My foundation is rational enough not to turn to things that are simply useless, even when panicking.

Edit 2: like a 1st round that works on non-anomalous humans, a 2nd that works on humans who have been trained with certain countermeasures, a 3rd that breaks the countermeasures against the 2nd, one that has worked on certain anomalous entities. Long. Painful. But with a purpose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no problem defending the idea that in the real world torture is obviously reprehensible, immoral, and, as you point out, utterly unreliable. In-universe, this is a life-or-death situation for the Foundation, and the narrative seeks to underline just how useless it is.

I dont agree that this is comparable to the gratuitous rape-content you refer to. By the way, 110-Mountauk spares the readers of any voyeuristic details, as do I. I'm not glorifying or morbidly focusing on the elements of torture. They're aren't any. What is implied, rather, is the psychological impact. Not the physical.

Mere containment can not be an option. The SCP, as I keep pointing out, has been masquerading for decades. Its imperative that any/all measures be employed to discover what it was doing, what it was up to. The Foundation realizes how horrible the situation is, but have no choice. Also, this is part of the narrative tension: the need to extract information, and the hopelessness of doing so given that they pair are indistinguishable. Perhaps the double doesn't know its the double. Perhaps it does. The Foundation knows that this may be unanswerable. But say the double has boobytrapped all sorts of Keter-class items. Say it has brethren in the O5 council. Say its in the thrall of some GOI. What would you do in that situation? To be clear, I don't mean this as a tacit endorsement of real-world equivalents like Guatanamo Bay, Abu Grhaib, etc. This is a story. Not advocacy. Also, as another commentator pointed out, this is the only way to safely ever release the real Doctor. Conceivably, they could give him amnesties to remove the memory of the torture.

—edit I just added this possibility in one of the transcripts.

I'm not sure what you refer to when you mention inconsistencies. Please let me know what you mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, that makes no goddamn sense. What exactly are they going to accomplish there? They've already tortured both copies into confessing that they were the fake. What could they possibly gain now? Since you have no means of verifying who the real person is, it doesn't matter if eventually the surviving specimen tells you that it's the fake. They have every motivation to give the answer that will make the pain stop. That's why torture isn't even justifiable from a logical perspective, it's an extremely unreliable means of obtaining information.

And also, this is absolutely ludicrous as an alternative to permanent containment. You think they're just going to spring Doctor So-and-So after brutalizing him for what could have been years? "Hey dude, sorry about that, no hard feelings right? Anyway, back to work with you, don't be a security risk or anything." They're likely going to have to contain him anyway for the rest of his life; the least they can do is to not fucking torture him forever for no possible good reason.

This to me is a cousin of when people shoehorn rape into their articles as a cheap means of dramatic effect. The actions of the Foundation are completely nonsensical in this context and don't make any sense when considered against the rest of the narrative. I really, really don't like it when people attempt to paper over inconsistencies and shortcomings in a story with shocking content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. Also for validating my own view on the interrogations. I had hoped I was making it clear the foundation wasn't just doing it for kicks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you line it and thanks for the edits. By the way. I'm going to keep the thaumiel Medal of Honor. I just like te way it sounds. I look forward to looking at your own skip. =D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't feel like needless torture to me. In this case, the Foundation knows that the only way to save the actual doctor from permanent containment is to determine which one that is. They went through a battery of medical tests before resorting to "interrogation," which is something the Foundation wouldn't be squeamish about, considering they regularly execute D-Class after their time is up. In fact, I think they'd be More likely to result to torture if it was to save one of their own.

I like this one a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, I really like this one, because the main hook of 'are you the real one or not' is one that I particularly enjoy (and employed in one of my own SCPs - SCP-1536 if you're interested), and the dialogue was also very good.

I went through and made quite a few minor edits for punctuation in the dialogues, so please check to make sure I haven't overstepped. I also edited your footnote to use Wikidot syntax, and finally altered the chess notation to have lower-case square references (as per en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algebraic_chess_notation).

Last note: the medal I've heard used most (especially posthumously) is the Foundation Award for Valour - although as there's no canon please feel free to ignore it if you prefer.

edit: forgot my own SCP number!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted.

Definitely improved from last attempt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seemingly needless torture aside, good article. I like the idea of questioning whether you are the original or the copy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted, but it could be better as a tale.

item.title: Re: re-initialization
item.content:encoded: I hadn't realized that. Thanks, Roget.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My belief is that given the skip has been undercover or masquerading for decades, the Foundation is understandably alarmed about what is probably the greatest security breach in its history.

They're not torturing them for fun. Its essential they 'break' it and get it to reveal everything it knowns, everything it may have done, whatever it in the thrall of, etc. Its also implied that there could well be others of them.

I think I'm depicting the Foundation not as heartless, but simply as ruthless. Not as sadistic, simply desperate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate that your response is thoughtful. I'll have to think about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well it must exist. There's a duplicate doctor.

-edit

its also implied that it may not know that its the impostor which I think turns the despairing nature of the situation into something somewhat existential. What is it to be an impostor if you react the same as the original. I'm not trying to be grandiose for its own sake…its just what I was attempting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why would the foundation do these things?

Faced with an apparent duplication, why would it assume some kind of infiltration? The CIA might be paranoid, the FBI might be incompetent, but the foundation can't afford to have unclear motives or sloppy reasoning - and they know it.

Who on the O5 or ethics committee is stupid enough to think torture is a way to get information? The foundation is cold as marble, but it isn't cruel, and it doesn't do pointless things on the off-chance that "Oh well, it might give us information, of some kind, somehow".

The premise is a good one - an old sci-fi staple. And the dialogue is snappy, and I think quite believable. I suggest it would be better as a foundation tale about an investigation into how the affair was mishandled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So basically it doesn't exist.

It's just the Foundation being paranoid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So now, instead of the "let's torture this dude" angle being an afterthought in an otherwise boring article, it's front and center, making this an actively bad article.

Between the completely unrealistic flippant attitudes of people being incarcerated, tortured and mindfucked for what's apparently at least months, the fact that the Foundation is torturing people for no good reason at all, and the dialogue ("this is kafkaesque, no it's more like dostoevsky dohoho"), I've gone from not really caring about this to wanting it off the site.

This one was 24 hours from cradle to grave.

In this case, it's showing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is really interesting, but it seems to be really similar to SCP-600 in the fact that they both take the forms of other people, but this one does it willingly. It seems cliche, but it has more "addon stuff" than description it's a bit more original.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh thank you very much. This one was 24 hours from cradle to grave. I'm trying to be less perfectionist in my writing.
Thanks again =D

-edit

Thanks also for suggesting the interrogation logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs a sweep through for a few grammar and tone slip-ups (I'm too tired to trust myself to edit anything atm) but I really enjoy this version. You didn't attempt to put forced feels in every sentence but still got across much of the hopelessness of the situation.

item.title: Re: re-initialization
item.content:encoded: You know you can rewrite without reposing, right? People might accuse you of clearing downvotes.

item.title: re-initialization
item.content:encoded:
I have re-worked this SCP using suggestions from the crowd. I believe its more substantial and interesting. Of course that for you to decide. I realize I've been using the main list as a sandbox. I will stop doing that.

-EDIT

In light of the uniform nature of the criticism, I have tried to address certain issues. I know its an arduous process, but for those who care to, please have a read through of the edited version.

And thanks for taking an interest =D

-Edit

One thing I'd love to have people's opinion on. Say the torture angle is still too harsh. What would be a more natural time line as far as the Foundation's response?

For instance:
1. initial alarm at the discovery of the two of them
2. quarantine
3. Nancy Y█████ is discovered absent.
3. colleagues commiserate. advocates are appointed to both of them
4. experimentation, medical, physical, psychological, etc.
5. longer term confinement.
6. interrogation….

then what? Or what other stages in between?
